q: After two atom bombs were dropped in Japan, what type of chemists were sent to study the radioactive chemicals and assess their effects on the environment?

feedback: Nuclear and environmental chemists study radioactive chemicals and assess their effects on the environment. Nuclear chemists study the properties of, and chemical reactions in, the nucleus of an atom, mainly in radioactive elements. Environmental chemists study the effect of different chemicals on the environment.

subject: Chemistry
q: Who will be able to classify chemicals as water or air pollutants, and analyse their concentrations in different substances?

Analytical chemists determine the types and the concentrations of chemicals present in different substances. Environmental chemists study the effect of different chemicals (e.g. air and water pollutants) on the environment.

subject: Chemistry

feedback: The large-scale production of sulphuric acid would require expertise in inorganic chemistry and industrial chemistry. Sulphuric acid does not contain carbon and carbon-hydrogen atoms, so it is NOT an organic compound. Since \[\ce{H2SO4}\] is an inorganic compound and large-scale production of chemicals by using technology and various chemical methods occurs in industries, large-scale production of sulphuric acid requires knowledge of both inorganic and industrial chemistry.

subject: Chemistry
q: Beryllium (Be) has an atomic number of 9 and a mass number of 4. Which of the following statements about Be is correct?
skill: Define the terms atomic mass, atomic mass unit, and relative atomic mass
feedback:
Be has 5 neutrons and 4 protons. The atomic number is the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. The mass number is almost equal to the number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom.

\begin{align} \text{mass number} &= \text{neutrons} + \text{protons} \\ \text{neutrons} &= \text{mass number} - \text{protons} \\ &= 9 - 4 = 5 \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q:
Fluorine (F) has a mass number of 19 and an atomic number of 9. Which of the following statements are correct?
  1. It has 9 neutrons and 10 electrons.
  2. It is 1.58 times as heavy as a C-12 atom.
  3. It has 9 protons.
skill: Define the terms atomic mass, atomic mass unit, and relative atomic mass
feedback:
II and III only are the correct statements.

Fluorine has 9 protons and 10 neutrons. The atomic number is the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom. The atomic mass is the relative mass of an atom compared to a C-12 atom. It is almost equal to the masses of both protons and neutrons. Therefore, the mass number contains the number of protons and neutrons present in the nucleus of an atom.

\begin{align} \text{mass number} &= \text{neutrons} + \text{protons} \\ \text{neutrons} &= \text{mass number} - \text{protons} \\ &= 19 - 9 = 10 \end{align}

Fluorine is 1.58 times as heavy as a C-12 atom. This is because its atomic mass is 19 amu due to the presence of 10 neutrons and 9 protons. One atomic mass unit (1 amu) is equivalent to 1/12 the mass of carbon-12 atom. Therefore, 19 amu is equivalent to 1.58 times the mass of a carbon-12 atom, as follows:

\begin{align} 1 \text{ u} &: \frac{1}{12}\\ 19 \text{ u} &: x\\ x &= 19 \times \frac{1}{12} = 1.58 \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the correct statement(s) about the atomic number and the atomic mass.
  1. The atomic number is the number of electrons in an atom.
  2. The relative atomic mass of any element is relative to the mass of a carbon-12 atom.
  3. The atomic mass is approximately equal to the mass of protons and neutrons in an atom.
skill: Define the terms atomic mass, atomic mass unit, and relative atomic mass
feedback:
II and III only are the correct statements about the atomic number and the atomic mass.

The atomic masses of all elements are measured in comparison to the mass of a carbon-12 atom. This is why it is called the relative atomic mass. The mass of C-12 is recorded at a highly accurate value of 12.000 grams.

The atomic mass is approximately equal to the mass of protons and neutrons that are present in all atoms.

The atomic number is the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom.

subject: Chemistry
q: Given that the mass of an unknown element is a quarter of the mass of a carbon-12 atom, what is its atomic mass?

feedback:
The atomic mass of the unknown element is 3.0 amu.

The mass of a carbon-12 atom is 12.0 amu. Since the mass of the unknown element is 1/4 of the mass of a carbon-12 atom, it is calculated below:

\begin{align} \text{mass of the new element} &= \frac{1}{4} \times 12.0 \\ &= 3.0 \text{ amu} \end{align}

subject: Chemistry

subject: Chemistry
q: Given that the relative atomic mass of Calcium (Ca) is 40 amu, which of the following statements is CORRECT?
options: A Ca atom is 40 times as heavy as a carbon-12 atom.

A Ca atom is 3.33 times as light as a carbon-12 atom.
skill: Define the terms atomic mass, atomic mass unit, and relative atomic mass
feedback:
The correct response is: A Ca atom is 3.33 times as heavy as a carbon-12 atom.

One atomic mass unit (1 amu) is equivalent to 1/12 of the mass of a carbon-12 atom. Therefore, 40 amu is equivalent to 3.33 (or 333%) times the mass of a carbon-12 atom:

\begin{align} 1 \text{ u} &: \frac{1}{12}\\ 40 \text{ u} &: x\\ x &= 40 \times \frac{1}{12} = 3.33 \text{ or 333%}. \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: The relative atomic mass of boron is 11 amu. What does this mean?

feedback:
A boron atom is 91.7% as heavy as a carbon-12 atom. One atomic mass unit (1 amu) is equivalent to 1/12 of the mass of a carbon-12 atom. Therefore, 11 amu is equivalent to 0.917 (or 91.7%) times the mass of carbon-12 atom, as follows:

\begin{align} 1 \text{ u} &: \frac{1}{12}\\ 11 \text{ u} &: x\\ x &= 11 \times \frac{1}{12} = 0.917 \text{ or 91.7%}. \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: A compound contains \[5.9\]% hydrogen (H) and \[94.1\]% oxygen (O). Its molecular mass is \[34.0\] g. What is its empirical formula? Hint: the atomic mass of H is \[2.0\] g and O is \[16.0\] g.
skill: Define and calculate empirical formula and molecular formula
feedback:
First, we will find the mass of H and O atoms of this compound as we already know their respective percentages and molecular masses.

\begin{align} \text{mass of H atoms} &= \frac{5.9}{100} \times (34.0) = 2.00 g\\ \text{mass of O atoms} &= \frac{94.1}{100} \times (34.0) = 32.0 g\\ \end{align}

Since 1 H atom weighs 1 g and 1 O atom weighs 16 g, we can find the total number of atoms of each element present in the compound.

\begin{align} \text{number of H atoms} &= \frac{2}{1} = 2 \\ \text{number of O atoms} &= \frac{32}{16} = 2 \\ \end{align}

Therefore, the molecular formula is \[\ce{H2O2}\]. Hence, the empirical formula is \[\ce{HO}\] as shown below.

\begin{align} \ce{H2} &: \ce{O2}\\ \frac{2}{2} &: \frac{2}{2}\\ 1 &: 1 \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: A compound contains \[43.7\]% phosphorus (P) and \[56.3\]% oxygen (O). Its molecular mass is \[284.0\] g. What is its empirical formula? Hint: the atomic mass of P is \[31.0\] g and O is \[16.0\] g.
skill: Define and calculate empirical formula and molecular formula
feedback:
First, we will find the mass of P and O atoms of this compound as we already know their respective percentages and molecular masses.

\begin{align} \text{mass of P atoms} &= \frac{43.7}{100} \times (284.0) = 124 g\\ \text{mass of O atoms} &= \frac{56.3 }{100} \times (284.0) = 160 g\\ \end{align}

Since 1 P atom weighs 31 g and 1 O atom weighs 16 g, we can find the total number of atoms of each element present in the compound.

\begin{align} \text{number of P atoms} &= \frac{124}{31} = 4 \\ \text{number of O atoms} &= \frac{160 }{16} = 10 \\ \end{align}

Therefore, the molecular formula is \[\ce{P4O_{10}}\]. Hence, the empirical formula is \[\ce{P2O5}\] as shown below.

\begin{align} \ce{P4} &: \ce{O_{10}}\\ \frac{4}{2} &: \frac{10}{2}\\ 2 &: 5 \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: The empirical formula of an unknown compound is \[\ce{C2H5Br}\]. Given that it contains 10 hydrogen (H) atoms, what is its molecular formula?
skill: Define and calculate empirical formula and molecular formula
feedback:
We are given the following two pieces of information about this unknown compound:
  • The empirical formula (which contains the simplest whole ratio of the atoms) is \[\ce{C2ClH4}\].
  • Since there are 10 H atoms, the molecular formula is \[\ce{C_{x}H_{10}Br_{y}}\], where \[x\] and \[y\] are unknown.
    • The mathematical relationship between the empirical and molecular formulae is as follows: \[Molecular formula = (Empirical formula)_n\], where \[n\] is a whole number

    Therefore, \[\ce{C_{x}H_{10}Br_{y}} = (\ce{C2H5Br})_{n}\].

    Since there are 10 H atoms in the molecular formula and 5 H atoms in the empirical formula, we can find \[n\]:

    \[n = \frac{10}{5} = 2\]

    Using \[n = 2\], we can find the molecular formula:

    \[\ce{C_{x}H_{10}Br_{y}} = (\ce{C2H5Br})_{2} = \ce{C4H10Br2}\].

subject: Chemistry
q: The empirical formula of an unknown compound is \[\ce{C2ClH4}\]. Given that it contains 2 chlorine (Cl) atoms, what is its molecular formula?
skill: Define and calculate empirical formula and molecular formula
feedback:
We are given the following two pieces of information about this unknown compound:
  • The empirical formula (which contains the simplest whole ratio of the atoms) is \[\ce{C2ClH4}\].
  • Since there are 2 Cl atoms, the molecular formula is \[\ce{C_{x}Cl2H_{y}}\], where \[x\] and \[y\] are unknown.
    • The mathematical relationship between the empirical and molecular formulae is as follows as: \[Molecular formula = (Empirical formula)_n\], where \[n\] is a whole number

    Therefore, \[\ce{C_{x}Cl2H_{y}} = (\ce{C2ClH4})_n\].

    Since there are 2 Cl atoms in the molecular formula and 1 Cl atom in the empirical formula, we can find \[n\] as follows:

    \[n = \frac{2}{1} = 2\]

    Now that we know \[n = 2\], we can find the molecular formula as follows:

    \[\ce{C_{x}Cl2H_{y}} = (\ce{C2ClH4})_2 = \ce{C4Cl2H8}\].

subject: Chemistry
q: The empirical formula of octanoic acid is \[\ce{C4H8O}\]. Given that octanoic acid contains 16 H atoms, what is its molecular formula?
skill: Define and calculate empirical formula and molecular formula
feedback:
We are given the following two pieces of information about octanoic acid:
  • The empirical formula (which contains the simplest whole ratio) is \[\ce{C4H8O}\].
  • Since there are 16 H atoms, the molecular formula is \[\ce{C_{x}H_{16}O_{y}}\], where \[x\] and \[y\] are unknown.
    • Recall that the mathematical relationship between empirical and molecular formulae is as follows as: \[Molecular formula = (Empirical formula)_n\], where \[n\] is a whole number.

    Therefore, \[C_{x}H_{16}O_{y} = (C_4H_8O)_n\].

    Since there are 16 H atoms in the molecular formula and 8 H atoms in the empirical formula, we can find \[n\] as follows:

    \[n = \frac{16}{8} = 2\]

    Now that we know \[n = 2\], we can find the molecular formula as follows:

    \[C_{x}H_{16}O_{y} = (C_4H_8O)_2 = C_8H_{16}O_2\]

subject: Chemistry
q: Butane is a gas used in cylinders for cooking. It contains 4 carbon (C) and 10 hydrogen (H) atoms. Its molecular formula is _____ and its empirical formula is _____.
skill: Define and calculate empirical formula and molecular formula
feedback:
Since butane contains 4 carbon (C) and 10 hydrogen (H) atoms, its molecular formula is \[\ce{C4H_{10}}\]. Empirical formulae give us the simplest whole number ratio of each type of atom. The simplest whole number ratio of \[4:10\] is calculated as follows:

\[\ce{C4:H_{10}}\]

\[\frac{4}{2}:\frac{10}{2} = 2:5\]

Therefore, the empirical formula of butane is \[\ce{C2H5}\].

subject: Chemistry
q: The molar mass of nitric acid (HNO<sub>3</sub>) is 63 g. What is the mass of 5.71 x 10<sup>22</sup> molecules of HNO<sub>3</sub>?
skill: Explain the chemical calculations based on the concept of mole and Avogadro's number
feedback:
1 mole of HNO3 contains 6.023 x 1023 molecules. We can find the number of moles that contain 5.71 x 1022 molecules as follows:

\begin{align} \mbox{moles} &: \mbox{molecules}\\ 1 &: 6.023 \times 10^{23}\\ x &: 5.71 \times 10^{22}\\ \end{align}

By cross-multiplying, we get:
\[(1)(5.71 \times 10^{22}) = (6.023 \times 10^{23})(x)\]

\[x = \frac{5.71 \times 10^{22}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} = 0.095 \text{ mol}\]

We also know that 1 mole of HNO3 has a mass of 63 g. Using this information, we can calculate the mass of 0.095 moles as follows:

\begin{align} \mbox{Mass} &= \mbox{Number of moles} \times \mbox{molecular mass}\\ &= 0.095 \times 63\\ &= 5.99\ g\\ \end{align}

Therefore, 5.71 x 1022 molecules of HNO3 have a mass of 5.99 g.

subject: Chemistry
q: The atomic mass of calcium (Ca) is 40 g. How many atoms of Ca are present in one glass of milk given that one glass contains 550 mg of calcium?
skill: Explain the chemical calculations based on the concept of mole and Avogadro's number
feedback:
The first step is to calculate the number of moles, \[x\], in 550 mg or 0.55 g of Ca using its molar mass:

\begin{align} \mbox{moles} &: \mbox{mass}\\ 1 &: 40\\ x &: 0.55 \\ \end{align}

By cross-multiplying, we get:
\[(1)(0.55) = (40)(x)\]

\[x = \frac{0.55}{40} = 0.0138 \text{ mol}\]

Now that we found the number of moles in 0.55 g of Ca, we can calculate the number of atoms as we know that 1 mole contains 6.023 x 1023 atoms:

\begin{align} \mbox{Atoms} &= \mbox{Number of moles} \times \mbox{Avogadro number}\\ &= 0.0138 \times (6.023 \times 10^{23})\\ &= 0.083 \times 10^{23} = 8.3 \times 10^{21} \text{ atoms}\\ \end{align}

Therefore, 550 mg (0.55 g) of Ca contains 8.3 x 1021 atoms.

subject: Chemistry
q: The molecular formula of glucose, which is a common sugar, is (\[\ce{C6H12O6}\]). How many molecules of glucose are present in a chocolate brownie that contains 100 g of sugar?
skill: Explain the chemical calculations based on the concept of mole and Avogadro's number
feedback:
The first step is to find the molecular mass of glucose. Since it has 6 C, 12 H and 6 O atoms, its molecular mass is calculated as follows:

\begin{align} \mbox{Molecular mass} &= 6(12.0) + 12 (1.0)+6(16.0) \\ &= 72+12+96\\ &= 180\ g\\ \end{align}

Now, we can calculate the number of moles, \[x\], in 100 g of glucose using its molecular mass:

\begin{align} \mbox{moles} &: \mbox{mass}\\ 1 &: 180\\ x &: 100 \\ \end{align}

By cross-multiplying, we get:
\[(1)(100) = (180)(x)\]

\[x = \frac{100}{180} = 0.56 \text{ mol}\]

Now that we found the number of moles in 100 g of glucose, we can calculate the number of molecules. 1 mole contains 6.023 x 1023 molecules:

\begin{align} \mbox{Molecules} &= \mbox{number of moles} \times \mbox{Avogadro number}\\ &= 0.56 \times (6.023 \times 10^{23})\\ &= 3.37 \times 10^{23}\\ \end{align}

Therefore, 100 g of glucose in a chocolate brownie contains 3.37 x 1023 molecules.

subject: Chemistry
q: How many atoms are present in 0.034 moles of gold (Au)?
skill: Explain the chemical calculations based on the concept of mole and Avogadro's number
feedback:
We know that one mole of any substance contains 6.023 x 1023 atoms. Using this information, we can calculate the number of atoms in 0.034 moles of Au.

\begin{align} \mbox{Molecules} &= \mbox{Number of moles} \times \mbox{Avogadro number}\\ &= 0.034 \times (6.023 \times 10^{23})\\ &= 0.20 \times 10^{23} = 2.0 \times 10^{22}\\ \end{align}

Therefore, 0.034 moles of gold (Au) contain 2.0 x 10 22 atoms.

subject: Chemistry
q: How many molecules are present in 3 moles of sodium hydroxide (NaOH)?
skill: Explain the chemical calculations based on the concept of mole and Avogadro's number
feedback:
We know that one mole of any substance contains 6.023 x 1023 molecules. Using this information, we can calculate the number of molecules in 3 moles of NaOH.

\begin{align} \mbox{Molecules} &= \mbox{Number of moles} \times \mbox{Avogadro number}\\ &= 3 \times (6.023 \times 10^{23})\\ &= 18.1 \times 10^{23} = 1.81 \times 10^{24}\\ \end{align}

Therefore, 3 moles of NaOH contain 1.81 x 10 24 molecules.

subject: Chemistry
q: Calcium hydroxide (Ca(OH)<sub>2</sub>) is an important chemical in making paper from wood. How many moles contain 0.35 x 10<sup>25</sup> molecules of Ca(OH)<sub>2</sub>?
skill: Explain the chemical calculations based on the concept of mole and Avogadro's number
feedback:
We know that one mole of any substance contains 6.023 x 1023 molecules. Using this information, we can calculate the number of moles, \[x\], that contain 0.35 x 1025 molecules of Ca(OH)2 as follows:

\begin{align} \mbox{moles} &: \mbox{molecules}\\ 1 &: 6.023 \times 10^{23}\\ x &: 0.35 \times 10^{25} \\ \end{align}

By cross-multiplying, we get:
\[(6.023\times 10^{23})(x) = (1)(0.35 \times 10^{25})\]

\[x = \frac{0.35\times 10^{25}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} = 5.8 \text{ mol}\]

Therefore, 5.8 moles contain 0.35 x 1025 molecules of Ca(OH)2.

subject: Chemistry
q: How many moles contain 9.75 x 10<sup>20</sup> molecules of carbonic acid (H<sub>2</sub>CO<sub>3</sub>)?
skill: Explain the chemical calculations based on the concept of mole and Avogadro's number
feedback:
We know that one mole of any substance contains 6.023 x 1023 molecules. Using this information, we can calculate the number of moles, \[x\], that contain 9.75 x 1020 molecules of H2CO3 as follows:

\begin{align} \mbox{moles} &: \mbox{molecules}\\ 1 &: 6.023 \times 10^{23}\\ x &: 9.75 \times 10^{20} \\ \end{align}

By cross-multiplying, we get:

\[(6.023\times 10^{23})(x) = (1)(9.75 \times 10^{20})\]

\[x = \frac{9.75\times 10^{20}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} = 0.0016 \text{ mol}\]

Therefore, 0.0016 moles contain 9.75 x 1020 molecules of carbonic acid (H2CO3).

subject: Chemistry
q: How many moles contain 2.34 x 10<sup>25</sup> molecules of sulphur dioxide (SO<sub>2</sub>) gas?

feedback:
We know that one mole of any substance contains 6.023 x 1023 molecules. Using this information, we can calculate the number of moles, \[x\], that contain 2.34 x 1025 molecules of SO2 as follows:

\begin{align} \mbox{moles} &: \mbox{molecules}\\ 1 &: 6.023 \times 10^{23}\\ x &: 2.34 \times 10^{25} \\ \end{align}

By cross-multiplying, we get:
\[(6.023\times 10^{23})(x) = (1)(2.34 \times 10^{25})\]

\[x = \frac{2.34\times 10^{25}}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} = 38.9 \text{ mol}\]

Therefore, 38.9 moles contain 2.34 x 1025 molecules SO2.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the correct statements about molar mass.

  1. Molar mass is the mass of one mole of a substance.
  2. Gram atomic, molecular, and formula masses are all different types of molar masses.
  3. Molar mass is measured in kilograms (kg).
skill: Define gram atomic mass, gram molecular mass, gram formula mass, formula unit, mole, Avogadro’s number and discuss their relationship with one another
feedback:
Molar mass is defined as the mass of one mole of a substance. There are three types of substances, atoms, covalent compounds, and ionic compounds, with three types of molar masses. Gram atomic mass for one mole of atoms, gram molecular mass for one mole of covalent molecules, and gram formula mass for one mole of formula units in ionic compounds.

Molar mass is measured in grams.

subject: Chemistry
q: The mass of one mole of a formula unit is called _____.
skill: Define gram atomic mass, gram molecular mass, gram formula mass, formula unit, mole, Avogadro’s number and discuss their relationship with one another
feedback:
Gram formula mass is the mass of one mole of the formula units of an ionic compound. Formula unit tells us the simplest ratio of atoms that exist in ionic compounds. For example, calcium chloride is an ionic compound that contains thousands of calcium and chloride ions. However, each calcium ion is bonded to only two chloride ions, so it has a formula unit of CaCl2.

Gram molecular mass is the mass of one mole of molecules of a covalent compound. Gram atomic mass is the mass of one mole of the atoms of an element. Atomic number is the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom.

subject: Chemistry
q: The mass of one mole of the molecules of a compound is called _____.
skill: Define gram atomic mass, gram molecular mass, gram formula mass, formula unit, mole, Avogadro’s number and discuss their relationship with one another
feedback:
Gram molecular mass is the mass of one mole of the molecules of a covalent compound. For example, the gram molecular mass of an oxygen (O2) molecule is 32 g. This means that the mass of the 6.023 x 1023 molecules present in one mole of oxygen is 32 g.

Gram atomic mass is the mass of one mole of the atoms of an element. Gram formula mass is the mass of one mole of the formula units present in an ionic compound. Atomic number is the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom.

subject: Chemistry

Gram atomic mass is the mass of one mole of an element. For example, the gram atomic mass of carbon is 12 g. It means that the mass of the 6.023 x 1023 atoms that make up one mole of carbon is 12 g.

Gram molecular mass is the mass of one mole of the molecules of a covalent compound.
Gram formula mass is the mass of one mole of the formula units that are present in an ionic compound.
Atomic number is the number of protons in the nucleus of an atom.

subject: Chemistry
q: Why did Rutherford say that an atom is a neutral particle?
options: It contains equal number of protons and electrons.
It contains equal number of protons and neutrons.
It contains equal number of electrons and neutrons.
There is not enough information provided to answer the question.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe Rutherford’s experiment leading to the discovery of atomic nucleus, and discuss its defects
feedback: Atom is a neutral particle because it contains equal number of protons and electrons.. While it is true that an atom may contain equal number of protons and neutrons, or electrons and neutrons, it does not explain that why an atom is neutral. Neutral means that there are equal number of positive and negative charges, or there are no charges at all. Since protons are positive and electrons are negative, an equal number of both makes an atom neutral.

skill: Describe Rutherford’s experiment leading to the discovery of atomic nucleus, and discuss its defects
feedback: \[\ce{He}\] atom is a noble gas, which is unreactive, and it contains two electrons. However, \[\ce{He^{++}}\] does not contain any electron. \[\ce{++}\] indicates that two electrons have been lost.

subject: Chemistry
q: Why did Rutherford use \[\ce{He^{++}}\] and not \[\ce{He}\] in his experiment?
options: \[\ce{He}\] is neutral and will not be deflected by the nucleus.
There is not enough information to provided to answer.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe Rutherford’s experiment leading to the discovery of atomic nucleus, and discuss its defects
feedback: \[\ce{He}\] is neutral and will not be deflected by the nucleus. . \[\ce{He}\] is a noble gas, which is unreactive. \[\ce{He}\] contains two electrons whereas \[\ce{He^{++}}\] does not contain any, which is why it is positively charged.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which observation could not have been made had Rutherford did not use a circular fluorescent screen to detect the reflection of alpha particles?
options: Electrons are much lighter than protons.
The mass of an atom is concentrated towards its centre.
Atoms consist of positively charged particles.
Atoms consist of negatively charged electrons.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe Rutherford’s experiment leading to the discovery of atomic nucleus, and discuss its defects
feedback: If Rutherford did not use a circular fluorescent screen, he would not have able to conclude that the mass of an atom is concentrated towards its centre . This conclusion was made after observing that very few alpha particles were completed reflected by the gold foil and detected on the part of the circular screen behind the foil. Rutherford argued that the nucleus of an atom, which is positively charged, must be in the centre of an atom. Hence, very few positively charged alpha particles were completely repelled by it.

subject: Chemistry
q:
According to Rutherford, electrons revolve around the nucleus like planets revolve around the sun. Why is this statement inconsistent with the energy emission spectrum of electrons?
  1. Electrons do not continuously emit enery.
  2. Electrons are located in fixed energy levels.
  3. Electrons release a fixed amount of energy as they jump from a lower to a higher energy level.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe Rutherford’s experiment leading to the discovery of atomic nucleus, and discuss its defects
feedback: I and II only are correct statements. Electrons are located in fixed energy levels or "shells" whose energy increases as the distance from the nucleus increases. To jump from a lower to a higher energy level, electrons need energy. However, electrons release a fixed amount of energy when they fall from a higher to a lower energy level. Therefore, a linear spectrum is observed. If electrons continuously revolved around the nucleus moving between the shells instead of remaining within one shell, they would release energy continuously giving rise to a continuous pattern, which is not observed in reality.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following statements does NOT indicate a defect in Rutherford's atomic model?
options: Electrons lose energy as they are accelerated to move in circular orbits.
The arrangement of electrons gives rise to linear spectrum.
Electrons will eventually collapse into a nucleus, making the atom unstable.
The arrangement of electrons gives rise to continuous spectrum.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe Rutherford’s experiment leading to the discovery of atomic nucleus, and discuss its defects
feedback:
Rutherford proposed that electrons revolved around the nucleus in circular orbits. However, if electrons moved in circular orbits, they would accelerate continuously since they would be changing directions. This continuous acceleration leads to a loss of energy. Therefore, electrons will eventually fall into the nucleus, making the atom unstable. However, we know that atoms are very stable; this is why, Rutherford's model is defective.

According to Rutherford, if electrons emit energy as they revolve, a continuous spectrum of energy emission would be observed. However, when the spectrum is experimentally determined, it is actually linear.

subject: Chemistry
q:
According to Rutherford, which of the following statements is true about the planetary model of an atom?

  1. Electrons orbit around the nucleus.
  2. Electrons orbit around the nucleus due to the electrostatic force of attraction.
  3. Electrons are attracted towards protons due to centripetal force.
  4. Centripetal and electrostatic forces are equal to each other.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe Rutherford’s experiment leading to the discovery of atomic nucleus, and discuss its defects
feedback: The planetary model of an atom proposed by Rutherford suggests that the mass of atoms is concentrated towards a dense, positively charged region in the centre. He named this region 'the nucleus'. He also suggested that electrons revolved around the nucleus in circular orbits due to centripetal force, NOT electrostatic force. The electrostatic force of attraction exists between positively charged nuclei and negatively charged electrons. Rutherford proposed that both centripetal and electrostatic forces are equal to each other.

subject: Chemistry
q: Rutherford noticed that few alpha particles were deflected when they passed through a gold foil. What conclusion can be drawn from this observation?
options: An atom possesses a positively charged region.
Electrons orbit around the centre of the atom.
An atom is made of neutrons, electrons, and protons.
The mass of an atom is spread throughout the nucleus.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe Rutherford’s experiment leading to the discovery of atomic nucleus, and discuss its defects
feedback:
• Rutherford noticed that most of the α particles went straight through the gold foil, and only a few were deflected. This means that an atom is mostly empty.
  • Rutherford also noticed that very few α particles were deflected by more than 90°. This means that the mass of atom is concentrated at its centre.
  • Since the deflection of α particles happen due to electrostatic repulsion between like charges, it means that the centre of an atom consists of a dense, positively charged region. Rutherford called this positively charged region 'the nucleus'.
  • Rutherford also noticed that because α particles were NOT deflected by electrons; they must be very light in mass. Therefore, electrons have a very low mass.

subject: Chemistry
q: What would happen if electrons began to emit light in their orbits?
options: Electrons could jump to higher energy level.
Electrons could fall to lower energy level.
There is not enough information to answer.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe the main points (postulates) of Bohr’s atomic model
feedback: When an electron emits light, it begins to lose energy that could cause it to fall to lower energy levels. Electrons never stop orbiting—they are always in motion, revolving around the nucleus.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following statements are true about Bohr's atomic model?

  1. Within any orbit, electrons remain in fixed positions.
  2. Electrons are continuously losing energy.
  3. Every orbit has a fixed amount of energy.
  4. Electrons that are closer to the nucleus have lower energy than electrons that are farther away.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe the main points (postulates) of Bohr’s atomic model
feedback:
Every orbit has a fixed energy, which is why they are called energy levels. Electrons in energy levels closer to the nucleus have less energy than those that are farther away from the nucleus. Emitting energy causes electrons to fall from a high energy level to a low energy level. Absorbing energy causes electrons to jump to a higher energy level. However, electrons do not lose energy continuously - it happens only if they emit light.

Within any orbit, electrons do NOT remain in fixed positions. They are always in motion. Also, electrons do NOT lose energy continuously. If they did, they would fall into the nucleus, making the atom unstable. We know that atoms are very stable.

subject: Chemistry
q: In order for an electron to go from energy level 3 to energy level 2, what needs to happen?
options: The electron needs to absorb light energy.
The electron needs to emit light energy.
The electron needs to revolve at a higher speed.
There is not enough information to answer.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe the main points (postulates) of Bohr’s atomic model
feedback: When an electron absorbs light, it gains enough energy to jump to a higher level. This is called the "excitation" of an electron. When an electron emits light, it falls down to a lower energy level, because it loses energy. Revolving at a higher speed does not cause the electron to jump from one energy level to another.

subject: Chemistry
q: In order for an electron to transition from energy level 1 to energy level 2, what needs to happen?
options: The electron needs to emit light.
The electron needs to revolve at higher speed.
There is not enough information to answer.
The electron needs to absorb light.
topic: Theories and experiments related to atomic structure
skill: Describe the main points (postulates) of Bohr’s atomic model
feedback: When an electron absorbs light, it can gain enough energy to jump to a higher level. This is called the "excitation" of an electron. When an electron emits light, it falls down to a lower energy level, because it loses energy. Revolving at a higher speed does not cause the electron to jump from one energy level to another.

subject: Chemistry
q: Given that magnesium (Mg) has three naturally occurring isotopes in abundance: Mg-24 (79%), Mg-25 (10%), and Mg-26 (11%),  what is its atomic mass?

feedback:

atomic mass \[= \frac{79}{100} \times 24 + \frac{10}{100} \times 25+ \frac{11}{100} \times 26\]

atomic mass \[= 18.96+ 2.5 + 2.86 = 24.3 \text{ amu}\]

atomic mass \[= \frac{20}{100} \times 10 + \frac{80}{100} \times 11\]

atomic mass \[= 2.0+ 8.8 = 10.8 \text{ amu}\]

subject: Chemistry
q:
Chlorine (Cl) has 17 protons and 18 neutrons. However, its atomic mass is 35.5 amu instead of 35 amu. Why?
  1. Chlorine has two naturally occurring isotopes: Cl-35 and Cl-37 in different abundances.
  2. Atomic mass of an element is the weighted average of the mass of all isotopes of the element.
  3. This is a mistake. The atomic mass of Cl is indeed 35 amu.
skill: Define isotopes and draw the structures of different isotopes
feedback:
I and II only is a correct statement. Many elements have isotopes, which is why the atomic mass of any element is the weighted average of the mass of all isotopes of the element. For example, Cl has two naturally occurring isotopes. 75% of all chlorine atoms are Cl-35, but remaining 25% are Cl-37. Therefore, the weighted average of the mass of Cl isotopes is calculated as follows:

atomic mass \[= \frac{75}{100} \times 35 + \frac{25}{100} \times 37\]

atomic mass \[= 26.25 + 9.25 = 35.5 \text{ amu}\]

subject: Chemistry
q:
Why do isotopes have different physical properties?
  1. They have different atomic masses.
  2. They have different number of neutrons.
  3. Physical properties depend on the number of electrons.
skill: Define isotopes and draw the structures of different isotopes
feedback: I and II only is a correct statement. Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have different atomic masses because they contain varying number of neutrons. For example, carbon-12 and carbon-13 are isotopes of carbon. Both contain 6 protons, but C-12 has 6 neutrons whereas C-13 has 7 neutrons. Therefore, C-13 is heavier than C-12. Due to differing mass, compounds containing C-12 and C-13 having varying mass, density, melting and boiling points etc., which are physical properties.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Which of the following statement(s) explains that why isotopes have same chemical properties?
  1. They have same number of protons.
  2. They have same number of electrons.
  3. Chemical reactivity depends on the number of protons.
skill: Define isotopes and draw the structures of different isotopes
feedback: II only is a correct statement. Chemical reactivity depends on the number of valence electrons. For example, atoms having eight valence electrons do not react at all whereas atoms having an unpaired valence electron (radicals) react very explosively. This is why isotopes have similar chemical properties since they contain equal number of valence electrons.

q: \[_{11}\]Na has electronic configuration _____.
skill: Determine the electronic configuration of 20 elements of the periodic table
feedback:
Sodium (\[_{11}\]Na) has the atomic number \[11\] so it has \[11\] electrons arranged in its atom.

The first two electrons of a sodium atom go in the \[1\]s orbital. Since \[1\]s can hold only up to two electrons the next two electrons go in the \[2\]s orbital. Following this, six electrons go in the \[2\]p orbital as it can hold a maximum of six electrons. Once these ten electrons have completed two shells, the one remaining electron fills up the \[3\]s subshell.

Therefore, the sodium electronic configuration will be \[1\]s\[^{2}\], \[2\]s\[^{2}\], \[2\]p\[^{6}\], \[3\]s\[^{1}\].

subject: Chemistry
q: Argon is present in the \[3^{rd}\] period of the periodic table. What is its electronic configuration and group number?

feedback:
Argon is a neon gas and is present in the \[3^{rd}\] period and \[8^{th}\] group of the periodic table. It has \[18\] electrons arranged in its shells.
An 's' subshell can hold up to \[2\] electrons and a 'p' subshell can hold up to \[6\] electrons. The \[18\] electrons in an argon atom thus fill up the \[1 s^{2}, 2 s^{2}, 2p^{6}, 3s^{2}, 3p^{6}\] shells.

subject: Chemistry
q: Carbon is present in group \[4\] of the periodic table. What is its electronic configuration and period number?

feedback:

The first two electrons of a carbon atom go in the \[1\]s orbital which can hold only up to two electrons, the next two go in the \[2\]s orbital. The remaining two electrons in a carbon atom then go in the \[2\]p orbital so that carbon has the electronic configuration \[ 1 s^{2}, 2 s^{2}, 2 p^{2}\] .

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct electronic configuration of an atom having an atomic number \['13'\] is _____.
skill: Determine the electronic configuration of 20 elements of the periodic table
feedback:
The electronic configuration of an atom is the distribution of electrons around its nucleus in various shells and subshells. Electrons fill the shells (starting from the first), and subshells (s, p, d and f) in order of their increasing energy with s being the subshell with the lowest energy carrying a maximum of \[ 2 \] electrons and p a higher energy level carrying up to \[ 6 \] electrons.

The first shell can hold only one s subshell and so a maximum of \[ 2\] electrons, the second shell can hold up to two subshells, s and p, and \[ 8 \] electrons while the third shell can hold three subshells: s, p and d.

The element with the atomic number \[ 13 \] is aluminium. Its correct electronic configuration thus is \[1\]S\[^{2}\], \[2\]S\[^{2}\], \[2\]P\[^{6}\], \[3\]S\[^{2}\], \[3\]P\[^{1}\].

subject: Chemistry
q:
Magnesium (Mg) belongs to group IIA and period 3 of the periodic table. Barium (Ba) belongs to group IIA and period 6. Identify the correct statement(s) about them.
  1. Ba has three more shells than Mg.
  2. Ba has three more valence electrons than Mg.
  3. Mg has a higher atomic number than Br.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback:
I only. The periods in a periodic table represent the number of shells that electrons occupy in an atom and the groups represent the number of valence electrons. Both Ba and Mg have two valence electrons i.e. the number of electrons in their outermost shells. However, Ba has six shells, which means that the electrons that are present in Ba atom occupy a total of six shells, whereas all electrons in Mg atom occupy only 3 shells.

Atoms have an equal number of protons and electrons, and the atomic number represents the number of protons. Therefore, we can conclude that Ba has a higher atomic number than Mg since electrons in Ba atom occupy three more shells than Mg atom.

subject: Chemistry
q: In a periodic table, elements of the same column or group have the same number of _____ and elements of the same row or period have the same number of _____.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: In a periodic table, elements of the same column have the same number of valence electrons and elements of the same row have the same number of shells. Valence electrons are the electrons that are in the outermost shell of an atom, also known as its valence shell. For example, Na and K are in group IA as both of them have one valence electron. However, Na has three shells and K has four shells. Therefore, Na is in period 3 and K is in period 4 of the periodic table.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Fluorine (F) and bromine (Br) belong to group VIIA of the periodic table. However, F belongs to period II and Br belongs to period IV. Identify the correct statement(s) about them.
  1. F has two valence electrons and Br has four.
  2. Both F and Br have seven shells.
  3. Atomic number of Br is greater than that of F.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback: III only. Since Br is in period IV and group VIIB, it has four shells and seven valence electrons. F has two shells and seven valence electrons. As Br has more shells than F, Br must as well have more electrons than F. In a neutral atom, the number of electrons is equal to the number of protons in its nucleus. Therefore, the atomic number of Br (which is the number of protons) is higher than that of F.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the correct statement(s) about elements that belong to the same period.

  1. They have the same number of valence electrons.
  2. They have the same chemical properties.
  3. They have the same physical properties.
options: None of the statements are correct.
skill: State the modern periodic law, and differentiate between a period and a group
feedback:
None of the statements are correct. Elements of the same period have the same number of shells. For example, carbon (C) and oxygen (O) belong to period 2 because both of them have two shells. However, C has four valence electrons and O has six. Therefore, their chemical properties are different. The number of valence electrons determines how an element reacts (if it does) and whether it forms ions or not.

Physical properties of elements depend on their atomic masses i.e. the number of protons and neutrons. Since C and O have atomic masses of 12 and 16 amu, respectively, they have different weights, volumes, densities, melting points, and boiling points.

feedback: Magnesium (Mg) is more reactive than aluminium (Al). Mg contains only two valence electrons whereas Al contains three. However, the valence electron of potassium is in the fourth shell whereas the valence electron of sodium is in the second shell. Therefore, it will take less energy to remove the valence electron of K as the fourth shell is farther away from the attraction of the nucleus. Since, reactivity depends on by the ability to lose electrons, K is more reactive as its ionisation energy is lower than that of Na.

Since, reactivity is defined by the ability to lose electrons, Na is more reactive than Ca.

options: All three of them are equally reactive.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback:
Calcium (Ca) is the most reactive metal. Reactivity of metals is determined by their ability to lose electrons. Metals that lose electrons easily, i.e. they have low ionisation energies, are more reactive than others. Calcium (Ca) has the lowest ionisation energy in the given list.

Even though Be, Mg, and Ca are group IIA alkaline earth metals; the ionisation of energy of Ca is the lowest since its valence electrons are the furthest away from the nucleus. Hence, Ca is the most reactive metal.

q: Why does the 4s sub-shell has less energy than the 3d sub-shell?
options: 4s-electrons experience less repulsion than 3d-electrons from electrons in 3s and 3p sub-shells.
4s-electrons experience more repulsion than 3d-electrons from electrons in 3s and 3p sub-shells.
skill: Determine the electronic configuration of 20 elements of the periodic table
feedback: 4s sub-shell has less energy than 3d sub-shell, because 4s-electrons experience less repulsion than 3d-electrons from electrons in 3s and 3p shell . This repulsion makes them unstable and increases their energy. Therefore, electrons prefer to reside in 4s subshell since it is further away from 3s and 3p subshells, which minimizes the repulsion.

subject: Chemistry
q: \[_{11}\]Na has electronic configuration _____.
skill: Determine the electronic configuration of 20 elements of the periodic table
feedback:
Sodium (\[_{11}\]Na) has the atomic number \[11\] so it has \[11\] electrons arranged in its atom.

The first two electrons of a sodium atom go in the \[1\]s orbital. Since \[1\]s can hold only up to two electrons the next two electrons go in the \[2\]s orbital. Following this, six electrons go in the \[2\]p orbital as it can hold a maximum of six electrons. Once these ten electrons have completed two shells, the one remaining electron fills up the \[3\]s subshell.

Therefore, the sodium electronic configuration will be \[1\]s\[^{2}\], \[2\]s\[^{2}\], \[2\]p\[^{6}\], \[3\]s\[^{1}\].

subject: Chemistry
q: Argon is present in the \[3^{rd}\] period of the periodic table. What is its electronic configuration and group number?
skill: Determine the electronic configuration of 20 elements of the periodic table
feedback:
Argon is a neon gas and is present in the \[3^{rd}\] period and \[8^{th}\] group of the periodic table. It has \[18\] electrons arranged in its shells.
An 's' subshell can hold up to \[2\] electrons and a 'p' subshell can hold up to \[6\] electrons. The \[18\] electrons in an argon atom thus fill up the \[1 s^{2}, 2 s^{2}, 2p^{6}, 3s^{2}, 3p^{6}\] shells.

subject: Chemistry
q: Carbon is present in group \[4\] of the periodic table. What is its electronic configuration and period number?
skill: Determine the electronic configuration of 20 elements of the periodic table
feedback:
Carbon is present in group \[4\] and period number \[2\] of the periodic table. It has a total of \[6\] electrons in its shells.
The first two electrons of a carbon atom go in the \[1\]s orbital which can hold only up to two electrons, the next two go in the \[2\]s orbital. The remaining two electrons in a carbon atom then go in the \[2\]p orbital so that carbon has the electronic configuration \[ 1 s^{2}, 2 s^{2}, 2 p^{2}\] .

subject: Chemistry
q: The correct electronic configuration of an atom having an atomic number \['13'\] is _____.
skill: Determine the electronic configuration of 20 elements of the periodic table
feedback:
The electronic configuration of an atom is the distribution of electrons around its nucleus in various shells and subshells. Electrons fill the shells (starting from the first), and subshells (s, p, d and f) in order of their increasing energy with s being the subshell with the lowest energy carrying a maximum of \[ 2 \] electrons and p a higher energy level carrying up to \[ 6 \] electrons.

The first shell can hold only one s subshell and so a maximum of \[ 2\] electrons, the second shell can hold up to two subshells, s and p, and \[ 8 \] electrons while the third shell can hold three subshells: s, p and d.

The element with the atomic number \[ 13 \] is aluminium. Its correct electronic configuration thus is \[1\]S\[^{2}\], \[2\]S\[^{2}\], \[2\]P\[^{6}\], \[3\]S\[^{2}\], \[3\]P\[^{1}\].

q: _____ is a measure of the tendency of an atom to attract electrons towards itself in a chemical bond.

feedback: Electronegativity is a measure of the tendency of an atom to attract electrons towards itself in a chemical bond. Fluorine (the most electronegative element) is assigned a value of 4.0, and values range down to caesium and francium which are the least electronegative at 0.7.

subject: Chemistry
q: The change in energy of a neutral atom when an electron is added to it to form a negative ion is called its _____.
skill: Define: shielding effect, electronegativity, atomic radii, electron affinity, and ionisation energy
feedback: Electron affinity is defined as the change in energy (in kJ/mole) of a neutral atom (in the gaseous state) when an electron is added to the atom to form a negative ion. It indicates the neutral atom's likelihood of gaining an electron.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which element in group I of the periodic table has the highest ionisation energy?
skill: Define: shielding effect, electronegativity, atomic radii, electron affinity, and ionisation energy
feedback: Ionisation energies in group I decrease as we move down the group, which means that lithium has the highest ionisation energy. This is due to the increased shielding effect as we move down the group, causing valence electrons to be more loosely bound to the atom.

subject: Chemistry
q: Alkali metals take part in chemical reactions readily. This suggests that they have _____ ionisation energies.
skill: Define: shielding effect, electronegativity, atomic radii, electron affinity, and ionisation energy
feedback: Alkali metals have low ionisation energies. This means they readily lose their valence electrons to form cations, which then take part in chemical reactions.

subject: Chemistry
q: The minimum amount of energy required to remove the outermost electron from an atom is called its _____.
skill: Define: shielding effect, electronegativity, atomic radii, electron affinity, and ionisation energy
feedback: The ionisation energy of an atom is the minimum amount of energy required to remove the outermost electron from a gaseous atom or ion. A high value of ionisation energy means there is a strong attraction between the nucleus and the outermost electron, thus requiring more energy to remove that electron. Conversely, a low ionisation energy means there is a weak attraction between the nucleus and the outermost electron.

subject: Chemistry
q: If we go from lithium to neon in the periodic table, the atomic size _____.
skill: Define: shielding effect, electronegativity, atomic radii, electron affinity, and ionisation energy
feedback: Lithium (Li) and neon (Ne) are found in the same row, and both have 2 electron shells. Li has atomic number 3, while Ne has atomic number 10. Even though Li has 1 valence electron and Neon has 8 valence electrons, the valence electrons of both elements are located in the same shell. Therefore, the electrostatic force of attraction by the nucleus is stronger in Ne than in Li due to higher number of protons in Ne. Hence, Ne has a smaller atomic size as its electrons are pulled more strongly towards the nucleus.

subject: Chemistry
q: The _____ is the average distance between the nucleus of an atom and the valence shell.
skill: Define: shielding effect, electronegativity, atomic radii, electron affinity, and ionisation energy
feedback: Atomic radius is the average distance between the nucleus and the valence shell where the outermost electrons are located. As the atomic number increases along each row of the periodic table, more electrons go into the same valence shell, but the number of protons increases. Therefore, the attractive force between nucleus and valence electrons increases, causing the atomic size to decrease.

q:

  1. Its atomic mass is 19 amu.
  2. It has 19 valence electrons.

skill: Deduce the group and period, and number of valence electrons of elements on the basis of electronic configuration
feedback:
The correct option is: III only. The element marked X is in period 4 and group IA of the period. It is potassium (K) which has a total of 19 electrons in its four shells, but only one valence electron in its fourth (N) shell. Therefore, its electron configuration is: \[\ce{1s^{2},2s^{2},2p^{6},3s^{2},3p^{6},4s^{1}}\].

Since all atoms have an equal number of electrons and protons, K has 19 protons as well. Therefore, 19 is its atomic number (which is the number of protons in an atom), NOT atomic mass.

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the correct electron configuration of chlorine (\[\ce{Cl}\]) after achieving the noble gas state?
skill: Discuss the importance of noble gas electronic configuration and the octet and duplet rules
feedback:
\[\ce{Cl}\] is a group VIIA element, which means it has 7 valence electrons, and it has three shells (as it is in the third period). It gains one electron in its outermost shell to achieve the noble gas electron configuration of argon (\[\ce{Ar}\]) that has eight electrons in its third shell.

\[\ce{Cl}\] = \[\ce{1s^{2}, 2s^{2}, 2p^{6}, 3s^{2}, 3p^{5}}\]

\[\ce{Cl}\] after gaining one electron and becoming a \[\ce{Cl^{-}}\] ion:

\[\ce{Cl^{-}}\] = \[\ce{1s^{2}, 2s^{2}, 2p^{6}, 3s^{2}, 3p^{6}}\]

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the correct electron configuration of magnesium (\[\ce{Mg}\]) after achieving the noble gas state?

feedback:
\[\ce{Mg}\] is a group IIA element, which means it has 2 valence electrons, and it has three shells (as it is in the third period). It loses two electrons from its outermost shell to achieve the noble gas electron configuration of neon (\[\ce{Ne}\]) that has eight electrons in its second shell.

\[\ce{Mg}\] = \[\ce{1s^{2}, 2s^{2}, 2p^{6}, 3s^{2}}\]

\[\ce{Mg}\] after losing two electrons and becoming a \[\ce{Mg^{2+}}\] ion:

\[\ce{Mg^{2+}}\] = \[\ce{1s^{2}, 2s^{2}, 2p^{6}}\]

q: Arrange the following compounds in the order of their boiling points from the lowest to the highest: \[\ce{HF}\], \[\ce{H2O}\], and \[\ce{NH3}\].
skill: Explain forces of interactions like dispersion forces, dipole-dipole interaction, hydrogen bonding
feedback:
\[\ce{NH3}\] < \[\ce{HF}\] < \[\ce{H2O}\] is the correct order. Boiling points of compounds depend on the strength of their intermolecular forces. Since all three compounds have H bonded to F, N, O atoms; they all form hydrogen bonds. Hydrogen bond is formed between a hydrogen atom in one molecule and a lone pair on the F/N/O atom in another molecule. Even though F contains 3 lone-pairs, \[\ce{HF}\] has only 1 H atom. One H atom can bond with only one lone pair. Therefore, on average, one molecule of HF can only form 2 hydrogen bonds. The first bond by donating its hydrogen atom and the second bond by accepting a hydrogen atom via its lone pair on F.

\[\ce{NH3}\] has 3 H atoms but only 1 lone pair on its N atom. One lone pair can accept only one hydrogen atom from another molecule. Therefore, like HF, it can only form 2 hydrogen bonds on average. The first bond by accepting a hydrogen atom via lone pair on N and the second bond by donating a H atom to another molecule of \[\ce{NH3}\]. However, F is more electronegative than N; hence, hydrogen bonds in \[\ce{HF}\] are more polar. Therefore, \[\ce{HF}\] has a higher boiling point that \[\ce{NH3}\].

\[\ce{H2O}\] has 2 H atoms and 2 lone pairs on its O atom. Therefore, it can form 4 hydrogen bonds. Two bonds by donating its 2 H atoms to another molecule of \[\ce{H2O}\] and two more by accepting hydrogen atoms via its 2 lone pairs. Therefore, as it forms twice as many hydrogen bonds compared to the other two compounds, it has the highest boiling point.

Compound D does not contain any polar covalent bonds, so it has the weakest intermolecular forces. Compound C contains only one \[\ce{-C-F -}\] polar covalent bond giving rise to dipole-dipole interactions. Compound A contains a \[\ce{-N-H -}\] polar covalent bond giving rise to hydrogen bonding, which is the strongest of all intermolecular forces. Compound B contains \[\ce{-O-H -}\] bond, which also gives rise to hydrogen bonding. However, since O is more electronegative than N, \[\ce{-O-H -}\] bond is more polar than \[\ce{-N-H -}\] bond. Therefore, the strength of hydrogen bonding in B is higher than in A, leading to a higher boiling point.

q: The pressure in a tyre on a cold morning was 30 psi and the volume of the tyre was 20 dm<sup>3</sup>. However, the day got hotter and the tyre expanded to 25 dm<sup>3</sup>. What is its new pressure?

feedback:
There is not enough information provided to answer. According to Boyle's law, an increase in volume will reduce pressure. The new pressure according to Boyle's law will be calculated as follows:

\begin{align} P_1V_1 &= P_2V_2\\ P_2 &= \frac{P_1 \times V_1}{V_2}\\ P_2 &= \frac{30 \times 20}{25}\\ &= 24\mbox{ psi}\\ \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: During winter and inside a room, a nitrogen balloon expands to a volume of 0.75 L at 21°C. Given that the temperature outside the house is 7.5°C, what will be the new volume of the nitrogen balloon if it gets released outside in the cold? You may assume that the pressure is kept constant.
skill: Discuss the temperature and volume changes in a gas using Charles’s law - Chemistry
feedback:
The new volume of the balloon outside the house will be 0.27 L. We know that the volume of gas increases when the temperature is increased and vice-versa. Since the temperature outside the house is lower than the temperature inside the house, the new volume must be less than 0.75 L. The exact value can be calculated using Charles' law:

\begin{align} \frac{V_1}{T_1} &= \frac{V_2}{T_2}\\ \frac{0.75}{21} &= \frac{V_2}{7.5}\\ V_2 &= \frac{0.75 \times 7.5}{21}\\ &= 0.27 \text{ L} \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: After heating a gas in an expandable syringe, at 54°C its volume was  87 dm<sup>3</sup>. If the initial temperature was 41°C, what was the volume of the gas prior to heating? You may assume that the pressure was kept constant.
skill: Discuss the temperature and volume changes in a gas using Charles’s law - Chemistry
feedback:
The initial volume of gas was 66.1 dm3. We know that volume of gas increases with increase in temperature and vice-versa. Therefore, prior to heating, the volume of gas must be less than 87 dm3. Using Charles' law, we can find the exact value since the pressure is kept constant:

\begin{align} \frac{V_1}{T_1} &= \frac{V_2}{T_2}\\ \frac{V_1}{41} &= \frac{87}{54}\\ V_1 &= \frac{87 \times 41}{54}\\ &= 66.1\ dm^{3} \end{align}

feedback:
The balloon will expand to 75 dm3 at 447 K. We know that the volume of gas increases when the temperature is increased and vice-versa. Therefore, the new temperature must be higher than 298 K. Using Charles' law, we can find the exact value since the pressure is kept constant:

\begin{align} \frac{V_1}{T_1} &= \frac{V_2}{T_2}\\ \frac{50}{298} &= \frac{75}{T_2}\\ T_2 &= \frac{75 \times 298}{50}\\ &= 447 \text{ K} \end{align}

subject: Chemistry
q: A table-tennis ball gets a small dent after being hit with a huge force. However, when the ball is submerged in a tub of <u>hot</u> water, it inflates, and the dent gets fixed. Why?
options: The air inside the ball contracts due to increase in temperature.
The air inside the ball expands due to increase in temperature.
The air inside the ball contracts due to decrease in pressure.
The air inside the ball expands due to decrease in pressure.
skill: Discuss the temperature and volume changes in a gas using Charles’s law - Chemistry
feedback: The air inside the ball expands due to the increase in temperature. When the ball is submerged in a tub of hot water, its temperature will increase. The increase in temperature will increase the volume of air inside the tennis ball in accordance with Charles' law. Therefore, air inside the ball will expand, forcing the ball to inflate and fixing the dent.

subject: Chemistry
q: The table below shows the volume and temperature of chlorine gas at constant pressure. Calculate the temperature at 270.0 ml given that the pressure is kept constant and its mass does not change.
skill: Discuss the temperature and volume changes in a gas using Charles’s law - Chemistry
feedback:
Since the pressure is kept constant, we can use Charles' Law to calculate the temperature of Chlorine when its volume is 270.0 ml. For that, we first need to calculate the constant in Charles' Law: \[k\]

\[V = k \times T \]
\[k = \frac{150}{135} = 0.9\]
Notice that we can use any combination of values to calculate the value of the constant since its value does NOT change when Charle's law is obeyed. For example, even using \[k = \frac{180}{200} = 0.90\] gives the same value of the constant as \[k = \frac{360}{400} = 0.90\].

Now that we have calculated the constant, we can use it to find the temperature at 270.0 ml.

\[T = \frac{V}{k} = \frac{270}{0.9} = 300^{\circ}C\]

q: Clothes are being hanged outside the house to dry on a windy but sunny day, and again on a still and sunny day. On which day will they dry faster?  You may assume that the same clothes are being dried on both days, and the outside temperature is constant.
options: There is not enough information provided to answer.
skill: Explain the properties of liquids and the factors that affect them
feedback: Clothes will dry out faster on a windy but sunny day. This is because as water will evaporate from the surface of clothes, the wind will take away those water molecules along with it. Therefore, the surface of clothes will not become saturated with water vapours and the rate of evaporation will increase. On a still and sunny day, due to the absence of wind, the surface of clothes will become saturated with water vapours, reducing the rate of evaporation.

q: Why do people feel cold when they come out after taking a shower?

The water decreases the blood pressure as it evaporates.
The water increases the blood pressure as it evaporates.
There is not enough information provided.
skill: Explain the properties of liquids and the factors that affect them
feedback: The water absorbs heat from the body's surface as it evaporates. Evaporation occurs when the molecules of liquid on the surface gain enough kinetic energy to leave as vapours, overcoming the intermolecular forces of attraction. The kinetic energy of water molecules on the surface of the human body rises as they absorb some of the body's heat. Therefore, people feel cold after taking a shower, because evaporation is also a cooling process as it takes away the body's heat.

The liquid whose molecules form dipole-dipole interactions will evaporate faster.

skill: Explain the properties of liquids and the factors that affect them
feedback: Evaporation occurs when the molecules of a liquid on its surface gain enough kinetic energy to escape the liquid as vapours, overcoming the intermolecular forces of attraction. Both hydrogen bonds and dipole-dipole interactions are types of intermolecular forces. However, hydrogen bonds are stronger than dipole-dipole interactions, which is why the liquid whose molecules form dipole-dipole interactions will evaporate faster.

feedback: I and II only are correct statements. When a liquid is boiled in an open container, its vapours can escape instead of colliding with the surface of the liquid. When a liquid is boiled on a lower flame i.e. at a lower temperature, the number of vapours decreases. Hence, both such measures reduce the vapour pressure. However, when a liquid is boiled in a close container, the vapours are unable to escape. More and more vapours collide with the surface of the boiling liquid, increasing its vapour pressure.

q: The melting point of iodine (\[\ce{I2}\]) is 114°C. What happens at the molecular level when iodine is heated until its melting point is achieved?
options: The covalent bonds between iodine atoms break.
The ionic bonds between iodide ions break.
The intermolecular forces between iodine molecules decrease.
There is not enough information provided.
skill: Describe the properties of solids
feedback: Iodine (\[\ce{I2}\]) is a covalent compound. The two atoms of iodine (I) are held together by a covalent bond (\[\ce{I-I}\]), which is an intramolecular bond. A solid block of iodine contains many molecules of \[\ce{I2}\], which are held together by intermolecular forces. When the block melts, the bond between the atoms (i.e. covalent \[\ce{I-I}\] bond) does NOT break. Instead, the distance between the molecules increases because of which intermolecular forces that hold all \[\ce{I2}\] molecules together, decrease. The molecules move even further apart, causing the conversion of the solid block into liquid as shown below.

feedback: \[\ce{CaO}\] has a higher melting point than \[\ce{Na2O}\]. Both are ionic compounds. Therefore, \[\ce{CaO}\] has a higher melting point than \[\ce{Na+}\].

q: What is the concentration of 134 g of ammonium chloride \[\ce{NH4Cl}\] dissolved in water (\[\ce{H2O}\]) to form a solution of 550 ml? Atomic masses are as follows: N: 14 g, Cl: 35.5 g, O: 16.0 g, and H: 1.0 g.
skill: Define and calculate percentage concentration of solutions in terms of proportion, ratio, or percentages of different solutions
feedback:
The concentration 134 g of ammonium chloride \[\ce{NH4Cl}\] dissolved in water (\[\ce{H2O}\]) to form a solution of 550 ml is 4.55 M.

Step 1. Calculate the number of moles of \[\ce{NH4Cl}\].

In order to calculate the number of moles of \[\ce{NH4Cl}\], we must know its molecular mass.

\begin{align} \text{molecular mass (g)} &= 14 + 4(1) + 35.5 = 53.5 \text{ g}\\ \\ \text{number of moles} &= \frac{\text{mass (g)}}{\text{molecules mass (g)}}\\ &= \frac{134}{53.5}\\ &= 2.50 \text{ mol} \end{align}

Step 2. Calculate the concentration of \[\ce{NH4Cl}\].

Note that the given volume must be first converted from millilitres (ml) to litres (L).

\begin{align} \text{volume of solution (L)} &= \frac{550}{1000} = 0.55 \text{ L}\\ \text{concentration (M)} &= \frac{\text{number of moles (mol)}}{\text{volume of solution (L)}}\times 100\\ &= \frac{2.5}{0.55}\\ &= 4.55 \text{ M} \end{align}

q: What is the concentration of 23 g of glycerol \[\ce{C3H8O3}\] dissolved in water (\[\ce{H2O}\]) to form a solution of 1250 ml? Atomic masses are as follows: C: 12 g, O: 16.0 g, and H: 1.0 g.

In order to calculate the number of moles of \[\ce{C3H8O3}\], we must know its molecular mass.

\begin{align} \text{molecular mass (g)} &= 3(12) + 8(1) + 3(16) = 92 \text{ g}\\ \\ \text{number of moles} &= \frac{\text{mass (g)}}{\text{molecules mass (g)}}\\ &= \frac{23}{92}\\ &= 0.25 \text{ mol} \end{align}

Step 2. Calculate the concentration of \[\ce{C3H8O3}\].

Note that the given volume must be first converted from millilitres (ml) to litres (L).

\begin{align} \text{volume of solution (L)} &= \frac{1250}{1000} = 1.25 \text{ L}\\ \text{concentration (M)} &= \frac{\text{number of moles (mol)}}{\text{volume of solution (L)}}\times 100\\ &= \frac{0.25}{1.25}\\ &= 0.2 \text{ M} \end{align}

\begin{align} \text{molecular mass (g)} &= 24 + 32 + 4(16) = 120 \text{ g}\\ \\ \text{Note that the given mass must also be in grams (g)}\\ \\ \text{mass of given }\ce{MgSO4} &= 0.3 \times 1000 = 300 \text{ g}\\ \text{number of moles} &= \frac{\text{mass (g)}}{\text{molecules mass (g)}}\\ &= \frac{300}{120}\\ &= 2.5 \text{ mol} \end{align}

q: The oxidation numbers of \[\mbox{Cr}\] in \[\mbox{Cr}_{2}\] \[\mbox{O}_{3}\] and \[\mbox{K}_{2}\] \[\mbox{Cr}_{2}\] \[\mbox{O}_{7}\] are _____.
skill: Define oxidation and reduction in terms of loss or gain of oxygen, hydrogen or electrons
feedback:
The oxidation state of an element in a compound represents the number of atoms lost or gained by its atoms in the formation of that compound.

In \[\mbox{Cr}_{2}\] \[\mbox{O}_{3}\], three oxygen atoms each lose two electrons to have a total oxidation state of \[ -6 \]. Since the final oxidation state of \[\mbox{Cr}_{2}\] \[\mbox{O}_{3}\] must be neutral,

\[ -6 + 2 \mbox{X} = 0 \], where X is the oxidation state of Cr.

\[ \mbox{X} = \frac{6}{2} = +3 \]

In K\[_{2}\]Cr\[_{2}\]O\[_{7}\], two potassium atoms give one valence electron each to obtain a total oxidation state of \[+ 2 \] and seven oxygen atoms each gain two valence electrons to obtain a total charge of \[ - 14 \].

Since the final oxidation state of K\[_{2}\]Cr\[_{2}\]O\[_{7}\] is neutral,

\[+2 + (-14) + 2 \mbox{X} = 0 \] where X is the oxidation state of Cr.

X = \[ \frac{12}{2} = +6\]

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following reactions is an example of oxidation?
options: a chlorine molecule to a chlorine ion
a silver atom to a silver ion
an oxygen molecule to an oxide ion
an iron (III) ion to an iron (II) ion
skill: Define oxidation and reduction in terms of loss or gain of oxygen, hydrogen or electrons
feedback:
Oxidation is the loss of electrons or an increase in oxidation state by a molecule, atom, or ion.
Silver atoms have an oxidation state of \[0\] and silver (I) ions have an oxidation state of \[+1\]. The conversion of silver atoms to silver (I) ions thus involves an increase in oxidation state and is an example of oxidation.

Reduction is the decrease in oxidation state by a molecule, atom, or ion. An iron (III) ion has an oxidation state of \[+3\] which decreases to \[+2\] as it changes to an iron (II) ion. A chlorine molecule's oxidation state decreases as it converts to a chlorine ion with an oxidation state of \[ -1\] and an oxygen molecule's oxidation state also decreases as it changes to an oxide ion with the oxidation state \[-2\]. All these decreases in oxidation state are examples of reduction.

q:
Which of the following elements in the reaction below is reduced?

\[ZnO\] + \[H_{2}\] → \[Zn\] + \[H_{2}O\]
skill: Define oxidation and reduction in terms of loss or gain of oxygen, hydrogen or electrons
feedback: Reduction is the gain of electrons or a decrease in oxidation state by a molecule, atom, or ion. Initially in the molecule \[ZnO\], the zinc ion has donated \[2\] electrons and has an oxidation state of \[+2\].  As the reaction takes place and the ZnO molecule loses oxygen and converts to Zn, its oxidation number becomes \[0\] (the oxidation number of any neutral element which contains atoms of only one kind is always \[0\]).  Therefore, \[ Zn\] reduces as after the reaction, its oxidation state decreases from \[+2 \] to \[0\].

q: In which of the following changes has the nitrogen atom been reduced?
skill: Define oxidation and reduction in terms of loss or gain of oxygen, hydrogen or electrons
feedback:
Reduction is the gain of electrons or a decrease in its oxidation state by a molecule, atom, or ion. A nitrogen atom N\[_{2}\] has an oxidation state of \[ 0 \]. In the compound NH\[_{3}\], three hydrogen atoms have each donated one electron to obtain a total oxidation state of \[ + 3 \]. Thus,
\[ \mbox{X} + (+3) = 0 \], where X is the oxidation state of nitrogen.
X = \[ -3 \]
A decrease in the oxidation state from \[0\] to \[-3\] means the nitrogen atom has been reduced.

Oxidation is the increase in the oxidation state by a molecule, atom, or ion. The oxidation state of nitrogen increases to \[+2\] in NO, to \[+4\] in NO\[_{2}\] and to \[+5\] in HNO\[_{3}\] and so the nitrogen atom is oxidised in these cases.

q: Bilal set up an incomplete galvanic cell without using a salt bridge. What will be the effect of it?
options: The cell will not produce any voltage.
The cell will produce voltage but only for a short period of time.
The cell would become unstable, creating an explosion.
There is not enough information provided to answer.
skill: Explain the working of a Daniel (galvanic cell) and determine the half-cells of oxidation and reduction - Chemistry
feedback: The cell will produce voltage but only for a short period of time. The purpose of a salt-bridge is to maintain a charge balance between the two half-cells. As electrons move from the anode to the cathode, the overall charge of the cathode compartment becomes negative and the anode compartment becomes positive. To minimise this charge difference, positively charged ions in the salt bridge move to the cathode compartment. Similarly, the negatively charged ions in the salt bridge move to the anode compartment. If the cell didn't have any salt bridge, the reaction would abruptly come to a halt as the current will stop flowing due to accumulation of positive charges around the anode and negative charges around the cathode.

q: Sara set up a galvanic cell and started the reaction. After a while, she noticed that the copper metal began to deposit at the zinc electrode. What did she do wrong?
options: Sara used copper sulphate instead of zinc sulphate as an electrolyte in the oxidation half-cell.
Sara used copper sulphate instead of zinc sulphate as an electrolyte in the reduction half-cell.
Sara did not use a salt bridge.
There is not enough information provided to answer.

feedback:
Sara used copper sulphate instead of zinc sulphate as an electrolyte in the oxidation half-cell.

A Daniell (or Galvanic cell) is made up of two-half cells: an oxidation half-cell where oxidation occurs and a reduction half-cell where reduction occurs. Oxidation half-cell contains a zinc electrode and zinc sulphate electrolyte. At the anode, zinc oxidises into \[\ce{Zn^{+2}}\] ions and releases electrons. Because Sara dipped the zinc electrode in a copper sulphate solution, copper ions (\[\ce{Cu^{+2}}\]) accepted those electrons and reduced to the atoms of copper metal, depositing on the zinc electrode.

Electrodes in both half-cells are made of two different metals. The electrode in a reduction half-cell is always made of a less reactive metal that acts as a cathode. q: Which of the following gases are produced in a dry cell?
options: There are no gases produced in a dry cell.
skill: Describe how a dry cell works as an example of a battery - Chemistry
feedback:
Both ammonia (\[\ce{NH3}\]) and hydrogen (\[\ce{H2}\]) gases are produced in a dry cell during reduction reaction at the cathode.

The reaction at the cathode happens in two parts:

1. The electrons from Zn anode reduce (NH4+) ions, producing ammonia and hydrogen gas. NH4+ ions come from NH4Cl in the electrolyte.

\[\ce{2NH4+ + 2e- -> 2 NH3 + H2}\]

2. The build-up of hydrogen gas can cause the dry cell to explode. Therefore, MnO2 is present in a dry cell and reacts with hydrogen gas forming water.

\[\ce{H2 + MnO2 -> Mn2O3 + H2O}\]

Therefore, the overall reaction at the cathode becomes:

\[\ce{2NH4+ + 2 MnO2 + 2e- -> Mn2O3 + 2 NH3 + 2H2O}\]

subject: Chemistry
q: If 786.1 g of fused NaCl was electrolysed in Downs cell and the extraction yield was 100%, how much mass of sodium metal was obtained? Atomic mass of Na = 23 g and Cl = 35.5 g.
options: There is not enough information provided to answer.
skill: Describe the manufacturing of sodium metal from fused NaCl
feedback:
The mass of obtained sodium will be 308.2 g. Since one mole of \[\ce{Na+}\] ions reduce to one mole of \[\ce{Na}\] atoms, we can find the mass by calculating the number of moles of \[\ce{Na+}\] ions in 786.1 g of \[\ce{NaCl}\].

\begin{align} \text{moles of sodium chloride} &= \frac{\text{mass in grams}}{\text{molar mass}}\\ &= \frac{786.1}{(23+35.5)} = \frac{786.1}{58.5}\\ &= 13.4 \text{ mol} \end{align}

Since one mole of \[\ce{NaCl}\] ionises into one mole of \[\ce{Na+}\] ions, the molar ratio is \[1: 1\].

\[\ce{NaCl -> Na+ + Cl-}\]

\[\ce{Na+ + e- -> Na}\]

\begin{align} \text{mass of sodium} &= \text{number of moles} \times \text{molar mass}\\ &= 13.4 \times 23 \\ &= 308.2 \text{ g} \end{align}

potassium hydroxide, chlorine gas, and hydrogen gas

feedback:

During electrolysis of aqueous potassium chloride (\[\ce{KCl}\]), water is ionised into \[\ce{H+}\] and \[\ce{OH-}\] ions and \[\ce{KCl}\] is ionised into \[\ce{K+}\] ions and \[\ce{Cl-}\] ions. Since the cathode is negatively charged, both \[\ce{K+}\] and \[\ce{H+}\] ions will migrate towards the cathode. However, since hydrogen is less reactive than potassium, \[\ce{H+}\] ions will easily reduce and combine together, forming hydrogen gas. Negatively charged \[\ce{Cl-}\] ions will migrate towards the positively charged anode. They will oxidise and combine together to form chlorine gas.

\[\ce{2Cl- -> Cl2 + 2e-}\]

Positively charged \[\ce{K+}\] ions will react with negatively charged \[\ce{OH-}\] ions in the solution, forming aqueous potassium hydroxide (\[\ce{KOH}\]).

q:
Why does aluminium (\[\ce{Al}\]) not corrode like iron (\[\ce{Fe}\]) during the rusting process?
  1. \[\ce{Al}\] reacts with water and oxygen to form aluminium oxide (\[\ce{Al2O3}\]), whereas \[\ce{Fe}\] forms iron oxide (\[\ce{Fe2O3}\])
    2. .
  2. \[\ce{Al2O3}\] is a weaker adhesive than \[\ce{Fe2O3}\]
    3. .
  3. \[\ce{Al}\] is less reactive than \[\ce{Fe}\].
skill: Define the term corrosion with examples, and discuss methods to prevent it
feedback:
I only is the correct response.

\[\ce{Al}\] is more reactive than \[\ce{Fe}\]. However, when it reacts with oxygen and water in the atmosphere, it forms \[\ce{Al2O3}\]. \[\ce{Al2O3}\] is a much stronger adhesive, and it adheres firmly to the surface of \[\ce{Al}\], protecting it from further corrosion. By In contrast, \[\ce{Fe2O3}\] does not adhere to the surface of \[\ce{Fe}\], instead, it falls off, exposing more \[\ce{Fe}\] to the corrosion.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following methods will NOT protect the iron tanks buried under the moist ground from corrosion? The reactivity of metals is: Mg > Al > Fe > Ag.
skill: Define the term corrosion with examples, and discuss methods to prevent it
feedback:
Iron tanks buried under the moist ground will NOT be protected from corrosion by connecting them with silver. Silver is less reactive than iron, whereas, other metals, such as magnesium, zinc, and aluminium, are more reactive than iron. Hence, even if iron is connected with silver, it will still readily oxidise and react with water and oxygen present in the atmosphere, thus forming rust or iron oxide (\[\ce{Fe2O3}\]).

This form of protection of metals is called cathodic protection. In this form, the metal that needs to be protected (iron tank) acts as the cathode by being connected to a more reactive metal (Mg or Al) that acts as the anode. The more reactive metal oxidises, thus, sacrificing itself for the less reactive metal.

Therefore, even after being coated with copper, iron will readily oxidise and react with water and oxygen in the atmosphere, thus, forming rust or iron oxide (\[\ce{Fe2O3}\]).

q: The chemical reactivities of calcium (Ca), aluminium (Al), copper (Cu), and gold (Au) are arranged as follows from the most reactive to the least reactive: Ca > Al > Cu > Au. Which of the following statements is CORRECT?

skill: Define the term corrosion with examples, and discuss methods to prevent it
feedback: Au can be protected from corrosion by coating it with Cu. Corrosion occurs when a metal oxidises by reacting with oxygen and water in the atmosphere to form metal oxides. A more reactive metal oxidises more readily than a less reactive metal. Therefore, corrosion of metals can be prevented by coating them with more reactive metals. Since Cu is more reactive than Au, Au should be coated with Cu to protect it from corrosion.

subject: Chemistry
q: _____ is the process of connecting a metal that needs to be protected to a reactive metal using an electric cable.
skill: Define the term corrosion with examples, and discuss methods to prevent it
feedback:
Cathodic protection is the process of connecting a metal that needs to be protected to a more reactive metal by using an electric cable.

The more reactive metals, for example, aluminium and magnesium, act as the anode and get oxidised, thus, decomposing into ions. Therefore, they sacrifice themselves to protect the less reactive metal. This form of protection is also known as sacrificial protection.
Without being connected to the more reactive metals, the metal that we are trying to protect gets oxidised itself. However, through cathodic protection, the corrosive reaction that was going to occur to that metal shifts to the more reactive metals that get sacrificed.
