skill: Explain electroplating of metals on iron and steel using examples
feedback:
The steel can will be plated with copper instead. During tin plating, the anode is made of tin \[\ce{Sn}\] metal and the steel can is used as the cathode. At the anode, \[\ce{Sn}\] will oxidise into \[\ce{Sn^{+2}}\] ions. However, since the electrolyte is \[\ce{CuSO4}\], it will ionise into copper ions (\[\ce{Cu^{+2}}\]) and sulphate ions (\[\ce{SO4^{-2}}\]). Therefore, at the cathode, instead of \[\ce{Sn^{+2}}\] ions, \[\ce{Cu^{+2}}\] ions will be reduced to \[\ce{Cu}\] atoms as copper is less reactive (easily reduced) than tin. Hence, copper will be deposited on the steel instead of tin. This is why the electrolyte must be made of the same metal as the anode during electroplating.

Anode: \[\ce{Sn -> Sn^{+2} + 2e-}\]

Cathode: \[\ce{Cu^{+2} + 2 e- -> Cu}\]

subject: Chemistry
q: During electroplating, the more reactive metal should be placed at the _____.
options: Not enough information is provided to answer.
skill: Explain electroplating of metals on iron and steel using examples
feedback:
During electroplating, the more reactive metal should be placed at the anode. The more reactive metal will readily lose electrons and oxidise. Oxidation of electrodes happens at the anode. For example, during tin plating of steel, tin is placed at the anode, because it is more reactive than steel. Tin oxidises into tin ions that migrate to the steel cathode, where they will reduce to tin atoms. This is how tin is deposited onto steel.

Anode: \[\ce{Sn -> Sn^{+2} + 2e-}\]

Cathode: \[\ce{Sn^{+2} + 2 e- -> Sn}\]

subject: Chemistry
q: When electroplating an object with chromium, which of the following happens?
options: Chromium atoms are oxidised at the cathode.
Chromium atoms are oxidised at the anode.
Chromium ions are oxidised at the cathode.
Chromium atoms are reduced at the cathode.
skill: Explain electroplating of metals on iron and steel using examples
feedback: During chromium plating, chromium atoms (Cr) oxidise to chromium ions (\[\ce{Cr^{+3}}\]) at the anode. These ions migrate to the cathode, where they are reduced to Cr atoms again. Note that the particles that are reduced are \[\ce{Cr^{+3}}\] ions, not Cr atoms.

subject: Chemistry
q: Student X has setup the following to electroplate a spoon with tin. Is there anything wrong with his setup?
options: The spoon and tin metal should switch electrodes.
skill: Explain electroplating of metals on iron and steel using examples
feedback: During electroplating, the plating metal is made the anode, while the plated object is made the cathode. In this example, the spoon must be the cathode —it should connect to the negative terminal of the battery — while the tin metal must be the anode.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which setup can be used to electroplate an object with zinc?
skill: Explain electroplating of metals on iron and steel using examples
feedback: During electroplating, the plating metal is made the anode, while the plated object is made the cathode. The electrolyte must contain ions of the plating metal. Therefore, to electroplate an object with zinc, the anode must be zinc metal, and the cathode must be the object. The electrolyte is potassium zinc cyanide solution, which ionises into \[\ce{Zn^{+2}}\] ions that will deposit on the object.

subject: Chemistry
q: In chromium plating of steel, why is steel first plated with copper or nickel, before it is plated with chromium?
options: Chromium adheres too strongly to steel.
Chromium does not adhere strongly to steel.
Chromium causes steel to rust faster.
skill: Explain electroplating of metals on iron and steel using examples
feedback: Chromium does not adhere strongly to steel, which means that any direct coating of chromium onto steel will peel off easily. Therefore, steel is usually first coated with copper or nickel, which allows chromium to stick more strongly.

subject: Chemistry
q: During chromium plating of a metal, which reaction takes place at the anode?
skill: Explain electroplating of metals on iron and steel using examples
feedback:
During electroplating, the plating metal is made the anode, while the plated object is made the cathode. Positive metal ions in the electrolyte are attracted to the cathode, where they discharge and deposit as a thin coat of elemental metal. Therefore, during chromium plating, chromium is made the anode. Chromium atoms ionise into the electrolyte through this reaction:

\[\ce{Cr <=> Cr^{+3} + 3e-}\]

At the cathode, the chromium ions deposit as chromium metal through this reaction: \[\ce{Cr^{+3} + 3e- <=> Cr}\]

subject: Chemistry
q: The valence electron configuration of magnesium (Mg) is \[\ce{3s^2}\] and aluminium (Al) is \[\ce{3s^2, 3p^1}\]. Based on your understanding of chemical and physical properties of alkali and alkaline earth metals, which metal is more reactive?
options: There is not enough information given.
Both Al and Mg are equally reactive.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback: Magnesium (Mg) is more reactive than aluminium (Al). Mg contains only two valence electrons whereas Al contains three. In order for Mg to react and form an \[\ce{Mg^{+2}}\] ion, it needs to lose two electrons only. However, Al needs to lose three electrons to form an \[\ce{Al^{+3}}\] ion. Therefore, the ionisation energy of three electrons of Al is higher. Hence, Al is less reactive than Mg.

subject: Chemistry
q: The reactivity of sodium (Na) is _____ that of potassium (K).
options: There is not enough information given.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback: The chemical reactivity of sodium (Na) is less than that of potassium (K). Both Na and K are alkali metals. However, the valence electron of potassium is in the fourth shell whereas the valence electron of sodium is in the second shell. Therefore, it will take less energy to remove the valence electron of K as the fourth shell is farther away from the attraction of the nucleus. Since, reactivity depends on by the ability to lose electrons, K is more reactive as its ionisation energy is lower than that of Na.

subject: Chemistry
q: The reactivity of sodium (Na) is _____ that of calcium (Ca).
options: There is not enough information given.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback: The chemical reactivity of sodium (Na) is more than that of calcium (Ca). Na is an alkali metal whereas calcium is an alkaline earth metal. Alkali metals are radicals that contain unpaired electrons, which is highly unstable. Therefore, the ionisation energy of alkali metals is less than that of alkaline earth metals. Since, reactivity is defined by the ability to lose electrons, Na is more reactive than Ca.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following among beryllium (Be), magnesium (Mg), and calcium (Ca) is the most reactive metal? The atomic numbers are as follows: Be = 4, Mg = 12 , Ca = 20.
options: All three of them are equally reactive.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback:
Calcium (Ca) is the most reactive metal. Reactivity of metals is determined by their ability to lose electrons. Metals that lose electrons easily, i.e. they have low ionisation energies, are more reactive than others. Calcium (Ca) has the lowest ionisation energy in the given list.

Even though Be, Mg, and Ca are group IIA alkaline earth metals; the ionisation of energy of Ca is the lowest since its valence electrons are the furthest away from the nucleus. Hence, Ca is the most reactive metal.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following among calcium (Ca), sodium (Na), potassium (K), and magnesium (Mg) is the most reactive metal? The atomic numbers are as follows: Na = 11, Mg = 12, K = 19, Ca = 20.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback: Potassium (K) is the most reactive metal. Reactivity of metals is determined by their ability to lose electrons. Metals that lose electrons easily, i.e., they have low ionisation energies, are more reactive than others. Potassium (K) has the lowest ionisation energy in the given list.

subject: Chemistry
q: The chemical reactivity of alkaline metals _____ down the group, and the reactivity of alkaline earth metals _____ down the group.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback: The chemical reactivity of alkaline metals increases down the group, and the reactivity of alkaline earth metals also increases down the group. The chemical reactivity of metals depends on their ionisation energies. Metals that have low ionisation energies lose electrons easily which means they react more easily. Since, the ionisation energy of both alkali and alkaline earth metals decreases down the group, their reactivity increases.

subject: Chemistry
q: The relationship between ionisation energy and chemical reactivity of metals is _____.
options: Ionisation energy and chemical reactivities are identical properties.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback: The relationship between ionisation energy and chemical reactivity of metals is inversely proportional. This means that metals that have lower ionisation energies are more chemically reactive. More reactive metals lose electrons easily because they have low ionisation energies. Hence, as the ionisation energy of metals decreases, their reactivity increases.

subject: Chemistry
q: The ionisation energy of calcium (Ca) is _____ that of potassium (K).
options: There is not enough information given.
skill: Identify elements as alkali metals and alkaline earth metals and differentiate between their ionisation energies
feedback: The ionisation energy of Ca is greater than that of K. Both K and Ca are present in the third period of the periodic table. However, K is an alkali metal, which contains one valence electron, but Ca is an alkaline earth metal, which contains two valence electrons. The atomic radius of Ca is smaller than that of K due to its higher nuclear charge. Therefore, the valence electrons of Ca experience a stronger attraction by the nucleus; hence, it will take more energy to remove them.

subject: Chemistry
q: What is the melting point of sodium?
skill: Describe the physical and chemical properties of sodium, calcium, and magnesium and discuss their uses in daily life
feedback: The melting is \[98\] \[^\circ\]C. It is lower than those of lithium but higher than those of the heavier alkali metals potassium, rubidium, and caesium, following periodic trends down the group.

q: Sodium metal has ____ electrons.
skill: Describe the physical and chemical properties of sodium, calcium, and magnesium and discuss their uses in daily life
feedback: Sodium is a chemical element with the symbol Na and atomic number 11 which means it has 11 electrons. It is a soft, silvery-white, highly reactive metal. Sodium is an alkali metal, being in group 1 of the periodic table, because it has a single electron in its valence shell.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is the less malleable metal?
skill: Describe the physical and chemical properties of sodium, calcium, and magnesium and discuss their uses in daily life
feedback: Malleability is the process in which substances can be beaten into thin sheets. Mostly metals are malleable, and non-metals are not. Here although sodium is a metal it is less malleable than iron. Hence the less malleable one is sodium.

q: Which one of the following metals is the lightest and floats on water?
skill: Describe the physical and chemical properties of sodium, calcium, and magnesium and discuss their uses in daily life
feedback: Lithium is the lightest metal and has density about half of water that's why it floats on water. Same goes for sodium and potassium, they both float on water too.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is not the use of silver (Ag)?
skill: Describe the inertness of noble metals (silver, gold and platinum) and discuss their commercial importance
feedback: Silver (Ag) is NOT used to make catalytic converters. In the exhaust system of cars, platinum metal is present in the catalytic converters to remove carbon monoxide, which is an air pollutant.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is not the use of platinum (Pt)?
options: to remove air pollutants from cars
skill: Describe the inertness of noble metals (silver, gold and platinum) and discuss their commercial importance
feedback:
Platinum is NOT used to to make antacid medicines. Milk of magnesia (\[\ce{Mg(OH)2}\]) is used as an antacid, because it is a basic compound. It neutralises the acidity in stomach.

Pt is used as a catalyst to make sulphuric acid in a reaction called "contact process". It is also used as an electrode in fuel cells. In the exhaust system of cars, it is present in the catalytic converters to remove carbon monoxide, which is an air pollutant.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following is not the use of gold (Au)?
skill: Describe the inertness of noble metals (silver, gold and platinum) and discuss their commercial importance
feedback: Gold is NOT used to make photography films. Silver (Ag) is used to make them because silver halides e.g. silver chloride (AgCl) are light-sensitive compounds. When they are exposed to light, a chemical reaction happens which darkens the film to produce an image.

subject: Chemistry
q: What are coins made of?
skill: Describe the inertness of noble metals (silver, gold and platinum) and discuss their commercial importance
feedback: Coins are made copper and nickel. Copper and nickel mix together to form alloys that are used to make coins.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following chemicals react with both gold (Au) and platinum (Pt)?
options: both milk of magnesia and aqua regia
neither milk of magnesia nor aqua regia
skill: Describe the inertness of noble metals (silver, gold and platinum) and discuss their commercial importance
feedback:
Only aqua regia reacts with both gold (Au) and platinum (Pt). Aqua regia is made of 3 parts by volume of hydrochloric acid (\[\ce{HCl}\]) and 1 part of nitric acid (\[\ce{HNO3}\]). It has a yellow-orange colour, and it is used to dissolve noble metals e.g. gold and platinum.

Milk of magnesia (\[\ce{Mg(OH)2}\]) is made of a highly reactive magnesium metal. The reactivity of Au and Pt metals is really low, which is why they cannot displace magnesium.

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following compounds do metals copper (Cu) and silver (Ag) NOT react with?
skill: Describe the inertness of noble metals (silver, gold and platinum) and discuss their commercial importance
feedback: Copper (Cu) and silver (Ag) are noble metals that do not react with hydrochloric acid (\[\ce{HCl}\]). Noble metals do not lose electron easily i.e. they are hard to oxidise. Therefore, Cu and Ag react with strong oxidising agents only e.g. nitric acid (\[\ce{HNO3}\]) and perchloric acid (\[\ce{HClO4}\]).

subject: Chemistry
q: Metals that are very difficult to oxidise and do not react readily are called _____.
skill: Describe the inertness of noble metals (silver, gold and platinum) and discuss their commercial importance
feedback:
Metals that are very difficult to oxidise and do not react readily are called noble metals.

Transition metals are those elements that contain electrons in d subshell. Alkali metals belong to the group IA in the period table and they contain only one valence electron. Alkaline earth metals belong to the group IIA in the period table and they contain only two valence electrons.

subject: Chemistry
q: Rank the acidic strength of the hydrogen halides HCl, HI, HBr, and HF from highest to lowest.
skill: Discuss reactions of halogens with group I and II elements and their compounds
feedback: All hydrogen halides are acids that release hydrogen ions (H+) when dissolved in water. However, the strength of acids depends on the size of the halide ions. As we go down the group, the atomic radius of halogens increases. When the size of halide ions increases, their acidic strength also increases. I- is bigger in size than Br-, which is bigger than Cl-. F- is the smallest in size. Therefore, HI is a stronger acid than HBr, followed by HCl and HF.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the oxidising agent in the reaction shown below.

\[\ce{Cl2 + MgBr2 -> MgCl2 + Br2}\]
skill: Discuss reactions of halogens with group I and II elements and their compounds
feedback: Halogens are also oxidizing agents that oxidize halide ions into halogen atoms. MgBr2 is a salt (ionic compound) that is composed of one Mg2+ and two Br- ions. In this reaction, Cl2 is the oxidizing agent that oxidizes Br- ions to Br atoms, which react together to form Br2. Since Cl2 is the oxidizing agent, it is reduced in the reaction.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the oxidising agent in the reaction shown below.

\[\ce{Br2 + 2 NaI -> 2 NaBr + I2}\]
skill: Discuss reactions of halogens with group I and II elements and their compounds
feedback: Halogens are also oxidising agents that oxidise halide ions into halogen atoms, which react with one another to form molecules. NaI is a salt (ionic compound) that is composed of one Na+ and one I- ion. In this reaction, Br2 is the agent that oxidises I- ions to form I atoms, which react together to form I2. Since Br2 is the oxidising agent, it is reduced in the reaction.

subject: Chemistry
q: Rank the strength of the oxidising agents of these four halogens: Cl<sub>2</sub>, Br<sub>2</sub>, F<sub>2</sub>, and I<sub>2</sub>.
skill: Discuss reactions of halogens with group I and II elements and their compounds
feedback: The halogens Cl2, Br2, F2, and I2 are all oxidising agents. However, the strength of oxidising agents decreases down the group. F2 is a stronger oxidising agent than Br2, followed by Cl2 and I2. Therefore, F2 can oxidise all other halide ions but Cl2 can only oxidise Br- and I- ions. Br2 can only oxidise I- ions. I2 cannot oxidise any other halide ions.

subject: Chemistry
q: Rank the chemical reactivity of the four halogens: Cl<sub>2</sub>, Br<sub>2</sub>, F<sub>2</sub>, and I<sub>2</sub> from most reactive to least reactive.
skill: Discuss reactions of halogens with group I and II elements and their compounds
feedback: The chemical reactivity of halogens decreases down the group. This is because their chemical reactivity is defined by their ability to complete their octet by gaining an electron. Since halogens are in group 7, they have seven valence electrons and they need only one more to complete their octet. Recall that the ability of an atom to attract a shared pair of electrons is termed as electronegativity. F2 is more electronegative than Cl2 followed by Br2 and I2.

subject: Chemistry
q: Halogens are _____ because they _____ metals to form salts.
skill: Discuss reactions of halogens with group I and II elements and their compounds
feedback:
Halogens are oxidising agents because they oxidise metals to form salts. They are themselves reduced in the chemical reaction. For example, chlorine gas (Cl2) reacts with the metal sodium (Na) to form a salt, sodium chloride (NaCl). In this reaction, the oxidation state of Cl reduces from 0 in Cl2 to -1 in NaCl. The oxidation state of Na increases from 0 to +1.

\[\ce{Na + Cl2 -> NaCl}\]

subject: Chemistry
q: Which of the following options correctly identify the colour of halogens?
skill: Discuss reactions of halogens with group I and II elements and their compounds
feedback: Option A is correct. F2 exists as a gas and has a pale yellow colour. Cl2 exists as a gas and has a greenish yellow colour. Br2 exists as a liquid and is reddish-brown. I2 exists as a blueish black solid.
